did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a machine learning framework involving a Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA) to optimize Support Vector Machine (SVM) parameters for the segmentation and classification of skin lesions and skin cancer. It also employs a Res-Unet architecture (integrating U-Net and ResNet) for lesion border segmentation and image inpainting for hair removal, utilizing datasets such as ISIC 2017 and PH2.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new machine learning framework using a Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA) to optimize Support Vector Machine (SVM) parameters for segmenting and classifying skin lesions. It details specific methodologies (FOA-SVM, Res-Unet), datasets used (ISIC 2017, PH2), and reports quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) in tables, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Segmenting and classifying skin lesions using a fruit fly optimization algorithm with a machine learning framework
authors: Amara S. A. L. G. Gopala Gupta
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated machine learning framework to segment and classify skin lesions and cancer, addressing the high mortality rate of melanoma. The methodology employs a Res-Unet architecture for lesion border segmentation and hair removal via image inpainting, followed by feature extraction and classification using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) optimized by a Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA). The system was trained on the ISIC 2017 database and validated using the PH2 database and the ISIC 2017 test set. Evaluation metrics included the Jaccard Index for segmentation and sensitivity, specificity, and Mathew’s correlation coefficient for classification. The proposed FOA-SVM model achieved Jaccard Index values of 0.772 on the ISIC 2017 test set and 0.854 on the PH2 dataset, demonstrating competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as the primary malignant class and positive category in the binary classification stage. The study focuses on detecting melanoma as the deadliest form of skin cancer.

One of the three specific skin conditions classified in the study. It is distinguished from lupus erythematosus in the second classification stage and from melanoma in the binary classification stage.

One of the three specific skin conditions classified in the study. It is distinguished from seborrheic keratosis in the second classification stage and from melanoma in the binary classification stage.

Referenced as the negative class in the binary classification of melanoma vs. others, and as 'others' in the binary classification of seborrheic keratosis vs. others. These represent images not belonging to the three primary target classes.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Res-Unet architecture for lesion border segmentation and image inpainting for hair removal. Performance is evaluated using the Jaccard Index and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Seborrheic Keratosis, Lupus Erythematosus)
all_task_types.details: The primary task is classifying skin lesions into specific categories using an FOA-SVM model. The study involves a three-stage classification process: binary classification of melanoma vs. others, binary classification of seborrheic keratosis vs. others, and classification of lupus erythematosus vs. others. Metrics include sensitivity, specificity, and Mathew’s correlation coefficient.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public database from the IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) global skin lesion categorization competition, used as the primary source for training the system and as a test set for validation.

use.validation: Used as a test set to verify the model (Jaccard Index of 0.772 reported).
use.testing: Used as a test set to verify the model.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset

use.validation: Used to verify the model (Jaccard Index of 0.854 reported).

datasets_used.name: Custom Compiled Database
datasets_used.description: A database compiled from multiple sources including DermQuest, DermIS, PH2 dataset, DermNZ, the '11k hands' database, and images provided by experts. It was balanced using layered sampling to contain 300 images per class for 6 classes.

use.training: Used to train the classification method after down-sampling to balance the database.
use.testing: Used to test the classification method.

datasets_used.description: A public database used for segmentation efficiency evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used in the classification stage to continuously alter SVM variables during internal variable optimization.
Used to estimate feature metrics and evaluate the kernel for feature selection/ranking.
Used to compute feature estimates for feature selection/ranking.

statistical_methods_used.method: Eigen-vector Centrality feature ranking and selection (ECFS)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to assign grades to each characteristic and rank features based on mutual information and Fisher criterion.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard Similarity Index, and Dice Similarity Coefficient.

ISIC 2017.notes: Used for training the system and as a test set for verification. The model achieved a Jaccard Index of 0.772 on the ISIC 2017 test set.

PH2.notes: Used to verify the model. The model achieved a Jaccard Index of 0.854 on the PH2 dataset.

Dermnet NZ.notes: Used for segmentation efficiency evaluation. Table 2 reports Min/Max/Total stats for Dermnet NZ (labeled as A): JSI Total 0.975, DSC Total 0.975. Table 3 reports detection metrics for Dataset A (50 training/50 testing): Accuracy 99.08%, Sensitivity 99.68%, Specificity 99.80%. Text mentions 99.08% accuracy, 99.69% sensitivity, and 99.80% specificity for Dermnet NZ with 60% training/40% testing split.

Xiangya Derm.notes: Used for segmentation efficiency evaluation. Table 2 reports Min/Max/Total stats for Xiangya Derm (labeled as B): JSI Total 0.973, DSC Total 0.982. Table 3 reports detection metrics for Dataset B (90 training/10 testing): Accuracy 99.55%, Sensitivity 98.99%, Specificity 98.13%. Text mentions 99.59% accuracy, 98.99% sensitivity, and 98.15% specificity for Xiangya Derm with 86% training/17% testing split.

Ham10000.notes: Used for segmentation efficiency evaluation. Table 2 reports Min/Max/Total stats for Ham10000 (labeled as C): JSI Total 0.973, DSC Total 0.988. Table 3 reports detection metrics for Dataset C (80 training/20 testing): Accuracy 98.92%, Sensitivity 98.10%, Specificity 99.15%. Text mentions 98.92% accuracy, 99.15% specificity, and 98.10% sensitivity for Ham10000 with 90% training/10% testing split.

Comparison with Baselines.notes: Table 4 compares the proposed FOA-SVM against Grey Level, K-clustering, Watershed, and Edge Detection methods. FOA-SVM achieved the highest scores across all metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: FOA-SVM (Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm optimized Support Vector Machine) for classification; Res-Unet (integration of U-Net and ResNet) for segmentation.

architecture_details.segmentation_model: Res-Unet, which integrates U-Net and ResNet frameworks. Used for lesion border segmentation and image inpainting for hair removal.
architecture_details.classification_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) optimized by Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA). FOA optimizes two crucial SVM variables: penalty component and kernel function width.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Handcrafted features (shape, color, texture) and Deep Learning features retrieved using a CNN model structure pre-trained on the ABCD rule. Mutual Information (MI) assessment used as a fusion rule. Eigen-vector Centrality Feature Selection (ECFS) and SVM-RFE used for feature ranking and selection.

training_details.optimizer: Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA) used to optimize SVM hyperparameters (penalty and kernel width).
training_details.training_schedule: 5-fold cross-validation used for internal variable optimization. Data augmentation applied (rotations of 90, 180, 270 degrees; shifts of 8 pixels left/right).

stages: Binary classification: Melanoma vs. Other skin disorders.
Binary classification: Seborrheic keratosis vs. Other conditions.
Binary classification: Lupus erythematosus vs. Other conditions.
classification_or_output_head.refinement: SVM-RFE (Support Vector Machine - Recursive Feature Elimination) used to refine classification by improving accuracy of incorrectly identified images.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: FOA-SVM (Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm optimized Support Vector Machine)
GA-SVM (Genetic Algorithm optimized Support Vector Machine)
PSO-SVM (Particle Swarm Optimization optimized Support Vector Machine)
LOA-SVM (Lion Optimization Algorithm optimized Support Vector Machine)
preprocessing_techniques: Colour constancy using the grey world consistency approach to correct uneven light intensity and normalize colour alterations.
Normalization of pixel intensity levels and dynamic range by removing average measured RGB values from the training and test image database.
Noise reduction using a Gaussian filter.
Hair removal using segmentation algorithms, thresholding, filtering techniques, and structural bottom hat filtration.
Use of circular structuring elements (structuring factor) for hair removal based on hair thickness (kernel radius) and morphological closure procedures.
Data augmentation via image rotations (90, 180, 270 degrees) and shifts (8 pixels left/right) to balance the dataset.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
Images were shifted by 8 pixels to the left and right.
A layered sampling strategy was used to balance the database, resulting in 300 images per class for 6 classes (Malignant, Benign, Psoriasis, Eczema, Acne, Healthy) for a total of 1800 images.
Randomized down-sampling was applied to the psoriasis group (which had the fewest photos) to balance the database.

conclusions: The proposed FOA-SVM framework accurately diagnoses skin lesions and cancer, providing clinicians with decision-making information.

The integrative approach using the Fruit Fly Optimization Algorithm (FOA) to optimize Support Vector Machine (SVM) variables improves accuracy and provides important data for more reliable classification compared to previous methods.
The Res-Unet architecture, which integrates U-Net and ResNet, effectively segments lesion borders and removes hair via image inpainting, significantly enhancing classification outcomes.
The proposed model achieved Jaccard Index values of 0.772 on the ISIC 2017 test set and 0.854 on the PH2 dataset, demonstrating performance competitive with state-of-the-art methods.

The FOA-SVM model outperformed baseline methods (Grey Level, K-clustering, Watershed, Edge Detection) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard Similarity Index (JSI), and Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC).
On the Dermnet NZ, Xiangya Derm, and Ham10000 datasets, the system achieved high performance metrics, including approximately 98% accuracy, 99% specificity, 96% sensitivity, 95% JSI, and 97.5% DSC.
The study addresses the limitation of small training data by utilizing a large database and data augmentation to prevent overfitting and ensure comprehensive model accuracy.
important_remarks: The abstract contains a significant factual error, stating the research aims to 'diagnose breast cancer' instead of skin cancer, indicating potential drafting or copy-paste errors in the manuscript.

The study relies on a custom-compiled database sourced from multiple heterogeneous origins (DermQuest, DermIS, PH2, DermNZ, '11k hands', and expert-provided images), which may introduce label noise and distribution bias compared to standardized benchmarks.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the reported performance metrics, limiting the assessment of statistical significance.

Performance metrics for the custom database and other datasets (Dermnet NZ, Xiangya Derm, Ham10000) are exceptionally high (e.g., >98% accuracy, >99% specificity), which may suggest data leakage, overly optimistic evaluation splits, or lack of rigorous external validation against state-of-the-art deep learning models.

The segmentation performance on the primary ISIC 2017 test set (Jaccard Index 0.772) is notably lower than the performance reported on other datasets (e.g., 0.975 on Dermnet NZ), highlighting potential dataset bias or generalization issues.

The methodology uses a hybrid approach combining handcrafted features (ABCD rule) and deep learning features, but the specific fusion mechanism and feature dimensionality are not fully detailed in the provided context.

The study addresses the limitation of small training data by using data augmentation (rotations and shifts) and a large custom database, but the specific class distribution of the primary ISIC 2017 dataset used for training is not explicitly detailed in the provided text.
